text: At a waterworks in northeastern Germany, we conducted experiments in the laboratory and in the column treatment system in situ to investigate the application of activated carbon filtration as well as ion exchange for removal of short- and medium-chain per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS). Within the range of tested PFAS, the sorption affinities were found to be distinctly lower for short- chain per- and polyfluoroalkyl carboxylic acids (PFCA) than for long-chain substances and short-chain per- and polyfluoroalkyl sulfonic acids (PFSA). Thus, in the contaminated groundwater matrix contaminated with a cocktail of PFAS, the short-chain substances PFBA and PFPeA are only removed efficiently by means of activated carbon filtration within a short column run time corresponding to 6000 and 11,000 bed volumes (BV), respectively. Only after a long-term run corresponding to 100,000 bed volumes or more the longer chain PFCA with carbon atoms of six or more are removed sufficiently from solution. The removal of the short-chain substances by ion exchange media is also limited to relatively short timeframes, i.e. 5000 bed volumes for PFBA, 10,000 bed volumes for PFPeA and 18,000 bed volumes for PFHxA. The latter is, depending on the extent of contamination of the ground water, still distinctly longer than those required for activated carbon filtration. Due to the higher material costs of the ion exchangers there is no economic advantage if these are used as one-time system. Regeneration of the ion exchangers with the possibility to use them several times would, however, represent an economically advantage option. However, regeneration of the elution step for removal of the long-chain substances requires addition of ethanol to the regenerating solution. On the other hand, PFBA and PFPeA can be eluted from the weakly basic ion exchange media in the absence of any organic solvents.
